assessment: Followup64 validated the separate CPU residual batch replay, HIP shell CSR, and HIP frame-force component paths at the post-shell frontier. Followup65 composes the native HIP frame and shell outputs with the spring term into a full residual batch and residual-difference JVP contract: residual diff is 1.162e-6 N, relative residual diff is 8.58e-12, relative JVP diff is 1.72e-15, and the estimated kernel lane is 5.43x faster than the CPU batch path. The physical residual gate is still open because the base residual remains 0.0336384785 N, above the 0.001 N gate, and the backend is still subprocess-composed rather than a persistent in-process Rust/HIP worker.
next_backend_step: Replace subprocess component composition with a persistent in-process Rust/HIP worker that keeps frame, shell CSR, state batch, spring, and external load buffers resident, then wire that worker into the Newton/Krylov residual/JVP call boundary.
next_solver_step: Use the faster residual/JVP lane for adaptive coupled shell membrane/bending/frame material-globalization directions; do not repeat saturated local shell row-correction, finite-difference row-submatrix, scaled-floor Krylov, or secant-history sweeps as configured.
claim_boundary: Backend replay validation only. This proves a composed full residual/JVP contract at the latest post-shell frontier, but it does not claim full 3D nonlinear equilibrium closure, residual gate pass, or production in-process ROCm/HIP residency.
